name: Two Sum
oneLiner: Initialize a HashMap<Integer, Integer>.  Loop through nums. Calculate complement = target - nums[i].  Check: Does map contain complement?  If yes, return new int[] {map.get(complement), i}.  If no, map.put(nums[i], i)

name: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock III
oneLiner: The 4 States: Track firstbuy, firstsell, secondbuy, secondsell.  Initial Values: firstbuy and secondbuy start at -prices[0] (or Integer.MIN_VALUE). Sells start at 0.  The Transitions (Max of doing nothing vs. acting today):  firstbuy = max(firstbuy, -prices[i])  firstsell = max(firstsell, firstbuy + prices[i])  secondbuy = max(secondbuy, firstsell - prices[i])  secondsell = max(secondsell, secondbuy + prices[i])  Pro-Tip: If you update them in reverse order inside the loop (secondsell first, down to firstbuy), you don't even need temporary variables!

name: Partition to K Equal Sum Subsets
oneLiner: Prerequisite Check: Total sum must be perfectly divisible by $K$. Each subset target is totalSum / k.Base Case 1 (Global Success): If k == 1, return true. (If $K-1$ buckets are perfectly filled, the remaining unused numbers must naturally sum to the target).Base Case 2 (Bucket Filled): If currsum == targetsum, transition to the next bucket by recursing with idx = 0, currsum = 0, and k - 1.Branching & Pruning: Loop from idx to the end of the array. Only process if visited[i] == false AND currsum + nums[i] <= targetsum.State Reset (Backtrack): Mark visited[i] = true and recurse with idx = i + 1. If the path returns false, undo the choice by setting visited[i] = false and continue the loop

name: Insert Delete GetRandom
oneLiner: Data Structures: You need a HashMap<Value, Index> for $O(1)$ lookup and an ArrayList<Value> for $O(1)$ random access.Insert: Add value to ArrayList, add (Value, ArrayList.size() - 1) to HashMap.The Remove Trick (Swap & Pop): >    * Get the target's index from the HashMap.Overwrite the target's position in the ArrayList with the last element in the ArrayList (list.set(index, lastElement)).Update the HashMap to reflect the last element's new index.Remove the last element from the ArrayList ($O(1)$ time).Remove the target from the HashMap.Random: Use Random.nextInt(list.size()) to pick an index.

name: Flatten Binary Tree to Linked List
oneLiner: State Passing: Use a custom Pair(head, tail) to pass the start and end of flattened subtrees upwards.  Traversal: Do a Post-Order traversal (lp = getanswer(left), rp = getanswer(right)).  The Rewire (4 Cases):  Leaf: Return (root, root).  No Left Child: Return (root, right.tail).  No Right Child: Move left to right (root.left = null; root.right = left.head), return (root, left.tail).  Both Children: Connect them! root.left = null, root.right = left.head, left.tail.right = right.head. Return (root, right.tail)

name: Floyd Warshall Algorithm
oneLiner: The 3 Loops: $K$ (intermediate), then $I$ (source), then $J$ (destination).The Core Logic: Try to route $I \rightarrow J$ through $K$.The Update: matrix[i][j] = min(matrix[i][j], matrix[i][k] + matrix[k][j]).🚨 THE INFINITY TRAP: If the problem uses -1 to mean "unreachable", never add to it directly.Check: if (matrix[i][k] != -1 && matrix[k][j] != -1) before you calculate the new distance.Update if the current matrix[i][j] == -1 OR if your new distance is smaller.

name: Construct Roads
oneLiner: Use Colour Grading Bipartite technique using DFS

name: Batches
oneLiner: 1. "Knows each other" = DSU. 2. Instead of a size[] array, create a strength[] array. Initialize it in the constructor (Watch out: Students are 1-indexed, but the given array is 0-indexed!). 3. The Core Trick: In the Union method, when you attach the smaller component to the larger root (ulp_v), add their strengths: strength[ulp_v] += strength[ulp_u]. 4. The Final Count: Loop 1 to N. A node is the boss of a batch if parent[i] == i. If it is the boss, check if strength[i] >= D and increment your answer

name: Coloring a Cycle Graph
oneLiner: Cycle Graph Chromatic Number: Even nodes = perfect alternating bipartite (2 colors). Odd nodes = collision at the end (3 colors). return 2 + (A % 2);

name: Check Bipartite Graph
oneLiner: Create a color[] array initialized to -1. 2. Loop through all nodes (to handle disconnected components). If unvisited, paint it 0 and start DFS. 3. The Core Trick: In DFS, paint unvisited neighbors with the opposite color using 1 - col[src]. 4. If a neighbor is already visited and has the same color as the current node (col[nbr] == col[src]), return false!

name: Palindrome Partitioning II
oneLiner: The FAANG Trap: Do not write a 1D recursive function that uses a while loop to check if a string is a palindrome. That creates an $O(N^3)$ algorithm that will hit TLE. You must split the problem into two distinct DP phases to achieve $O(N^2)$.Phase 1: The 2D Palindrome Cache (The Lookup Table): * Use the "Gap Strategy" (diagonal traversal) to build a boolean[][] isPal table.isPal[i][j] is true if the substring from i to j is a palindrome. This gives you $O(1)$ lookups for Phase 2.Phase 2: The 1D Cuts Array (The State Definition): * cuts[i] represents the minimum number of cuts needed to perfectly partition the prefix string from index 0 to i.The Partition Transition:Base Case: If isPal[0][i] == true, the whole prefix is a palindrome. It needs 0 cuts! (cuts[i] = 0).The Cut Loop: If it's not a palindrome, try placing a cut at every index j (from 1 to i).If the right side of the cut is a palindrome (isPal[j][i] == true), then the total cuts = the cuts needed for the left side plus one new cut.cuts[i] = Math.min(cuts[i], cuts[j-1] + 1)

name: Palindromic Substrings Count
oneLiner: The State Definition: dp[i][j] is a boolean representing whether the exact substring starting at index i and ending at index j is a palindrome.The Gap Traversal: You cannot traverse row-by-row or column-by-column. You must traverse by length (gap) because a string of length 5 relies on the pre-calculated string of length 3 inside it.The Base Cases: * gap == 0 (Length 1): Single letters are always palindromes (true).gap == 1 (Length 2): Palindrome only if A[i] == A[j].The Recursive Transition: For any length $\ge 3$, the substring is a palindrome ONLY IF the outer characters match (A[i] == A[j]) AND the inner substring is also a palindrome (dp[i+1][j-1] == true).

name: Russian Doll Envelopes
oneLiner: The Object Mapping: You cannot separate heights and widths. Always map the 2D array into a custom Pair or Envelope object array so they travel together during sorting.  The Timeline Sort: To reduce the 2D problem into a 1D LIS problem, sort the array by one of the dimensions (e.g., Height ascending). This guarantees that any envelope j before envelope i is at least short enough to potentially fit inside i.  The Strict 2D LIS Check: Run the exact LIS nested loops, but your inner if condition must strictly check both dimensions. Equal sizes do not fit inside each other! if (env[j].w < env[i].w && env[j].h < env[i].h)  The Global Max: Just like standard LIS, the biggest nested doll doesn't necessarily end with the very last envelope in the array. Track ans = Math.max(ans, dp[i])

name: Longest Increasing Subsequence
oneLiner: The State Definition: dp[i] represents the length of the longest strictly increasing subsequence that ends exactly at index i.  The Base Case Initialization: Every single element is technically an increasing subsequence of length 1 all by itself. So, initialize the entire dp array to 1 (or set dp[i] = 1 + max1 like your code did).  The Look-Back Transition: To find the answer for index i, you must look back at every previous index j (from 0 to i-1).  If the number is strictly smaller (A[j] < A[i]), you can append A[i] to that sequence.  Update state: dp[i] = Math.max(dp[i], dp[j] + 1)  The Global Max Trap: The longest subsequence doesn't necessarily end at the very last index of the array. Never just return dp[n-1]. You must track a global maximum variable (ans = Math.max(ans, dp[i])) across the entire array.

name: Edit Distance
oneLiner: That base case bounds swap is the final boss of Edit Distance. Now that you have navigated the 1-indexed string shift and the unique Row 0 / Col 0 initializations, this architecture is fully yours.  Here is your 4-step SRS cheat sheet for the Edit Distance problem. Drop this directly into your notes:  The "Edit Distance Tabulation" SRS Cheat Sheet The State Definition: dp[i][j] represents the minimum number of operations required to convert the first i characters of String A into the first j characters of String B.  The Base Case Trap: Size your table as new int[N+1][M+1]. Unlike LCS, Row 0 and Column 0 cannot be 0.  Row 0: Converting an empty string to String B takes j inserts (dp[0][j] = j).  Col 0: Converting String A to an empty string takes i deletes (dp[i][0] = i).  The Match Transition: Shift your string indices back by one (A.charAt(i-1) == B.charAt(j-1)). If the characters match, no operation is needed. Inherit the cost from the diagonal: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1]  The Mismatch Transition: If the characters do not match, you must perform exactly 1 operation. Take the minimum cost of the three possible paths and add 1:  Insert: Look Left (dp[i][j-1])  Delete: Look Up (dp[i-1][j])  Replace: Look Diagonal (dp[i-1][j-1])  dp[i][j] = 1 + Math.min(Replace, Math.min(Insert, Delete))

name: Longest Common Subsequence
oneLiner: The State Definition: $dp[i][j]$ represents the length of the Longest Common Subsequence using the first $i$ characters of String A and the first $j$ characters of String B.The 1-Indexed Grid Trap: Size your table as new int[N+1][M+1] so row 0 and column 0 (representing empty strings) naturally default to $0$. When checking characters inside the loop, you must shift back by 1: A.charAt(i-1) == B.charAt(j-1).The Match Transition: If the characters match, you successfully found a common letter. Add 1 to your score and move diagonally up-left to check the rest of both strings:$dp[i][j] = 1 + dp[i-1][j-1]$The Mismatch Transition: If the characters do not match, you cannot take them. You must test skipping the current character of String A or skipping the current character of String B. Take the maximum of those two paths:$dp[i][j] = \max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1])$

name: Period of a string (KMP-LPS)
oneLiner: The Core Concept: The "Period" of a string is the minimum distance k between repeating characters, where $A[i] == A[i \% k]$.The LPS Secret: The last value of the LPS array (LPS[N-1]) tells you the length of the longest prefix that perfectly overlaps with the suffix of the entire string.The Visual Overlap: If a prefix of length $L$ perfectly matches a suffix of length $L$, it means every character in the overlap is exactly $N - L$ steps away from its identical twin.The Golden Formula ($O(N)$): Run KMP to generate the LPS array for the string. The minimum period is always strictly mathematically defined as: $k = N - LPS[N-1]$

name: Permutations of A in B
oneLiner: The Architecture (Fixed Size): Because any valid permutation of A must be exactly the length of A, the window size never changes. You do not need a while loop to shrink it.The Initial Setup: Create freq_A and freq_B arrays (size 26). Fully populate freq_A, but only populate freq_B with the first A.length() characters of string B. Check if they match using Arrays.equals(); if yes, count++.The Slide ($O(N)$): Loop from i = A.length() to the end of B. Slide the rigid frame by doing exactly two operations:Add Right: Increment the frequency of the new character entering the window: B.charAt(i).Drop Left: Decrement the frequency of the old character falling out of the window: B.charAt(i - A.length()).The Array Index Trap: When dropping the left character, do not accidentally use the raw index math (i - A.length()) inside the frequency array. You must fetch the actual character from string B first, then subtract 'a' to get the correct frequency bucket.

name: Cyclic Permutations -KMP
oneLiner: The "Cyclic Shift = KMP" SRS Cheat SheetThe XOR Decoy: X ^ Y = 0 mathematically guarantees that X == Y. The problem is simply asking: "How many cyclic shifts of B are exactly identical to A?"The Cyclic Trick (B + B): Never use Frequency Maps for cyclic shifts, as they ignore relative character order. To instantly generate every possible cyclic shift of a string, concatenate it to itself. Your new search space is Text = (B + B).substring(0, 2 * N - 1).The Architecture Shift: The problem has now been cleanly converted from a bit-manipulation/permutation disguise into a classic String Pattern Matching problem: Find how many times Pattern A appears inside Text.The KMP Execution ($O(N)$): Create the master string S = A + "#" + Text. Generate the LPS array for S. Iterate through the LPS array, and every time lps[i] == A.length(), increment your count.

name: Node distance C in binary tree
oneLiner: The Architecture (Pass 1): Traverse the tree to find the Target node AND build a HashMap<Node, Node> mapping every child to its parent (the "Up" pointer).  The Graph BFS (Pass 2): Start a Level-Order Queue from the Target node. You now have three valid moves: left, right, and parent.  The Infinite Loop Trap: You MUST use a HashSet to track visited nodes, otherwise you will bounce between child and parent forever.  The Extraction: Increment a distance counter after every full level. When distance == C, break the loop; the queue now contains exactly your answer.

name: Boring substring
oneLiner: The Core Secret (The Bridge): Odd ASCII characters are never adjacent to each other. Evens are never adjacent. If you separate them into two halves, you just need to find one valid pair (a bridge) to connect the Odd half to the Even half.The Trap (Brute Force - $O(N^2)$): Iterating through every single Odd character and comparing it against every single Even character will cause a Time Limit Exceeded (TLE) on massive strings.The HashSet Pivot ($O(N)$ Time, $O(1)$ Space): Since there are only 26 letters, dump characters into two HashSets (Odds and Evens). This reduces the nested loop to a maximum of $13 \times 13 = 169$ constant-time checks.The Masterpiece ($O(N)$ Time, $O(1)$ Space): Ditch the sets. Traverse the string once to find four extremes: minOdd, maxOdd, minEven, and maxEven. Test the 4 cross-combinations. If the absolute difference of any pair is != 1, return true. Otherwise, return false.

name: Flatten Binary Tree to Linked List
oneLiner: The Architecture: Use a recursive function returning a (head, tail) pair to flatten subtrees bottom-up.  The Wiring: Connect them by setting root.right = left_head, then left_tail.right = right_head.  The Trap: Always explicitly sever the left pointer (root.left = null) to prevent invalid tree cycles.  The Edge Case: If a node only has a left child, the true end of that list is the left_tail, not the left_head

name: Maximum XOR Subarray
oneLiner: you don't invent new logic; you capitalize on the rule that XOR is its own inverse by building a prefixXOR array and feeding it directly into your first Trie, with the vital upgrade of dropping "index anchors" at the leaf nodes. When a match is found, you pull those two indices to define your subarray boundaries (Left = min + 1, Right = max), which immediately allows you to filter the results through Scaler's strict three-tier tie-breaker: highest XOR first, then shortest length, and finally the earliest starting index.

name: Maximum XOR
oneLiner: the core engine is treating integers as 32-bit paths (MSB to LSB) in an empty Bitwise Trie, allowing you to make the "Greedy Choice" by always searching for the opposite bit (1 - bit) to guarantee the highest possible value

name: Auto Complete
oneLiner: To build an optimized Autocomplete system, upgrade the standard Trie node to store an ArrayList of the "Top 5" heavily weighted words. During insertion, at every single character node you step on, add the current word and its weight to that node's list, sort the list by weight descending, and remove the 6th element if the size exceeds 5. During the search phase, traverse down the prefix. If the path breaks, return -1. If you reach the end of the prefix, simply print the cached Top 5 list sitting inside that final node, completely avoiding the need for a costly DFS traversal.

name: Shortest Unique Prefix
oneLiner: To find the shortest unique prefix, upgrade your standard Trie node to include an int freq counter. During the insertion phase, increment this frequency for every single node you step on to track exactly how many words share that specific path. Then, to find the unique prefix for a word, traverse the Trie again and stop the exact moment you land on a node where curr.freq == 1. The substring from the root down to that specific node is mathematically guaranteed to be your shortest unique prefix.

name: Spelling Checker
oneLiner: To build a spellchecker, use a Trie to store the valid dictionary. First, iterate through the array of valid words and use standard Trie insertion to build the tree, ensuring the final character of each word is marked with eow = true. Then, iterate through the query array and run a standard Trie search for each word. The critical trap to avoid: your search function must return the final node's eow flag instead of a hardcoded true to guarantee the queried word is a complete dictionary word and not just a prefix.

name: Trie Deletion
oneLiner: The secret to deletion is finding the "Last Safe Node" so you don't accidentally delete nodes shared by other words. As you traverse down the target word, constantly update a temp pointer and reference character (rc) whenever the current node has more than 1 child or its eow is true (indicating an overlap). Once you reach the end of the target word, immediately set its eow to false. Finally, if that last node has 0 children, go back to your temp node and sever the dead branch by setting temp.children[rc - 'a'] = null

name: Search in Tries
oneLiner: To search for a word, start at the root and traverse character by character using the ch - 'a' array index. If at any point the child node you need evaluates to null, the path is broken, and you must immediately return false. If you successfully reach the final character of the word, do not just return true; instead, return the final node's eow flag to guarantee you found a complete, intentionally inserted word and not just a prefix of a longer word

name: Insertion in Tries
oneLiner: To insert a word into a Trie, start at the root node and iterate through each character of the string. Calculate the array index using ch - 'a', and if that specific child node is null, initialize a new node in that slot. Move your current pointer down into that child node and repeat the process for the next character. Once the loop finishes, you must mark the final node's eow (end of word) flag as true to signal that a complete word ends there.

name: Sudoku
oneLiner: The Architecture: Unlike N-Queens (which finds all solutions and returns void), Sudoku only needs one solution. The recursive function must return a boolean to instantly kill the search and bubble true up the call stack the moment the board is solved. The Traversal: Use a double for loop to scan the entire 9x9 board. If a cell is not '.', skip it. If it is '.', loop through '1' to '9'. The Logic (Do -> Recurse -> Undo): If a number is valid, set it (board[r][c] = guess). Recurse: if (solve(board) == true) return true;. If it fails, undo it (board[r][c] = '.'). The 3x3 Validator Math: To check if a guess is valid in its 3x3 block, find the top-left anchor using modulo math: start_row = row - (row % 3) and start_col = col - (col % 3). Then loop 0 to 2 and add them to the anchor

name: Word Break
oneLiner: The Architecture: Convert the dictionary to a HashSet for $O(1)$ lookups. Use a Boolean[] dp array of the same length as the string to store whether a breakdown is possible from a specific index. The Logic: Loop i from idx + 1 to A.length(). If dict.contains(A.substring(idx, i)) AND the recursive call canBreak(i) is true, return true. State Cleanup: Remember, because we are passing a new index i into the recursive function and not modifying a global string/array, there is no "undo" backtracking required!

name: NQueens
oneLiner: If a problem asks if ANY valid state exists (e.g., Sudoku), return boolean from your recursive calls to instantly kill the search once found. If a problem asks to generate ALL valid states (e.g., N-Queens), keep the return type void. Let the recursion naturally unfold and save the state only inside your base case

name: Trailing Zeros in Factorial
oneLiner: Don't calculate the factorial (overflow). Count the pairs of (2, 5). 2s are abundant, so just count the 5s using a loop: count += n / i where i multiplies by 5 each step.

name: Pubg- Minimum Health
oneLiner: Repeated subtraction between elements to find the absolute minimum remaining value is just asking for the GCD of the entire array

name: Compute nCr % p
oneLiner: You cannot divide under a modulo! To compute (A / B) mod M, turn division into multiplication using Fermat's formula: (A * B^{M-2})mod M

name: Very Large Power
oneLiner: To handle a massive exponent like $B!$, first shrink the exponent by calculating it modulo $P-1$ (k = fact(B, P - 1)). Then, apply standard binary exponentiation to the base using the new shrunken exponent: power(A, k, P)

name: Jump Game 2
oneLiner: Move right-to-left. At index i, look ahead at the dist array within your jump range (arr[i]). Find the smallest number of jumps remaining (minReachableDist). Then, add 1 to account for your current physical jump: dist[i] = minReachableDist + 1

name: Nearest Smaller Element
oneLiner: Use a Stack. For every element, run a while loop to pop() all elements from the stack that are >= the current element (don't forget !st.isEmpty()). Once the tall elements are cleared, if the stack is empty, answer is -1. If not, answer is st.peek(). Finally, always push() the current element onto the stack for the next iteration

name: Shortest Distance in a Maze
oneLiner: Because the ball rolls multiple spaces at once, the 'step cost' varies, meaning standard BFS fails. Use Dijkstra's algorithm (a PriorityQueue sorted by distance and a 2D distance matrix initialized to infinity), and for each direction, use a while loop to roll the ball until it hits a wall, pushing the final resting spot into the queue only if it offers a strictly shorter pat

name: Number of islands
oneLiner: Iterate through the grid. Only increment your island count and trigger a DFS when you find a cell that is both unvisited AND equals 1 (land). Inside the DFS, immediately return if out of bounds, if it's water, or if already visited, then crucially mark the current cell as visited before recursively calling all 8 directions.

name: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stocks II
oneLiner: Don't manually track peaks and valleys. Use a Greedy approach: loop from index 1 to N, and anytime A[i] > A[i-1], simply add A[i] - A[i-1] to your total profit. Every upward daily step contributes to the total

name: Dijkstra
oneLiner: Use a Priority Queue (Min-Heap) storing (node, total_weight_from_source) and a distance array initialized to -1. Continuously pop the minimum weight node, and if it hasn't been visited, lock in its distance and push all its neighbors into the queue with their new cumulative weights.

name: Topological Sort - Kahn's Algorithm
oneLiner: Calculate the in-degree for all nodes and push those with an in-degree of 0 into a Queue (use a PriorityQueue if you need the lexicographically smallest order). Pop nodes, add them to your answer array, and decrement their neighbors' in-degrees—pushing any neighbor that drops to 0 into the queue—and finally check if the answer size matches the total nodes to ensure no cycles exist

name: Possibility of Finishing
oneLiner: It is simple cycle detection using DFS, if cycle is present , then not possible or else it is possible

name: Another BFS
oneLiner: To handle weight-2 edges, insert a dummy node in the middle of each to convert them into two weight-1 edges. Then, run a standard BFS from the source and return the distance the moment you reach the destination.

name: Rotten Oranges
oneLiner: Use Multi-source BFS by initially pushing all rotten oranges into a queue with minutes = 0 while counting the total fresh oranges. Process the queue to rot valid 4-directional neighbors, pushing them back into the queue with minutes + 1. Return the final minutes, or -1 if any fresh oranges remain at the end.

name: Construction Cost
oneLiner: Convert the Edge List into an Adjacency List first. Use a Min-Heap (Priority Queue) to greedily pick the minimum weight edge that connects an unvisited node to the growing tree. Always maintain a visited array to prevent cycles, and ensure the Priority Queue is initialized with a custom comparator for the weights

name: Commutable Islands
oneLiner: Convert the Edge List into an Adjacency List first. Use a Min-Heap (Priority Queue) to greedily pick the minimum weight edge that connects an unvisited node to the growing tree. Always maintain a visited array to prevent cycles, and ensure the Priority Queue is initialized with a custom comparator for the weights

name: Cycle in Directed Graph
oneLiner: Use two boolean arrays: visited (global memory to avoid redundant work) and pathVisited (active path memory to detect cycles). When entering a node, mark both as true; if you ever encounter a neighbor where pathVisited is already true, you have found a cycle! When backtracking out of a node, set pathVisited to false (picking up your breadcrumb), but leave visited as true to ensure you never process that node again.

name: Path in Directed Graph
oneLiner: To find if a path exists from node $X$ to node $Y$, you do not need complex return logic. Simply initialize a global visited array, run a standard void DFS starting exclusively from node $X$, and afterward, check if visited[Y] == true. If it is, a path exists.

name: Connected Components in an Undirected Graph
oneLiner: Build the adjacency list, then loop through all vertices from $0$ to $A-1$. Whenever you hit a visited[i] == false, create a new empty list and pass it into a void dfs to collect all connected nodes. Once the DFS finishes, sort the collected list and add it to your final result array.

name: Cutting a Rod
oneLiner: For recursion and memoization, use a 1D state solve(length) with a base case of 0 when length == 0, and loop through all possible cuts i to return the maximum of price[i-1] + solve(length - i). For tabulation, invert this using a 1D array dp[N+1] with an outer loop i building the rod length from 1 to $N$, and an inner loop j trying every cut up to i, updating via the transition dp[i] = Math.max(dp[i], price[j-1] + dp[i-j])

name: 0-1 Knapsack II - Optimised
oneLiner: Treat the outer loop i as introducing a "new item" and the inner loop j as testing every possible "bag size," where the array index always represents the capacity. Read the transition equation as a physical choice for the current bag size j: you either skip the item and keep yesterday's best profit (prev[j]), or pack it by adding its value to yesterday's best profit for the leftover space (prev[j - weight])

name: Coin Sum Infinite
oneLiner: Use a 2D array where dp[i][j] represents the ways to make sum j using coins up to index i. The state transition is dp[i][j] = (dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j - coin]) % 1000007, ensuring you handle base cases where sum 0 is 1 way, and keeping the "take" value at 0 when the coin exceeds the current target sum.

name: 0-1 Knapsack
oneLiner: Create a dp[n][capacity + 1] array and initialize the 0th row to the first item's value whenever the capacity allows (j >= weight[0]). For the remaining items, the max value at dp[i][j] is the maximum of either skipping the item (dp[i-1][j]) or taking it if it fits (value[i] + dp[i-1][j - weight[i]])

name: Unbounded Knapsack
oneLiner: The implementation is nearly identical to 0/1 Knapsack, but when you pick an item, you stay on the current row i instead of moving back to i-1 so the same item can be selected again. Calculate dp[i][j] as the maximum of either skipping the item completely (dp[i-1][j]) or taking it if capacity allows (value[i] + dp[i][j - weight[i]])

name: Amazing Subarrays
oneLiner: Every time a vowel is found at index i, it acts as the starting character for exactly length - i substrings. Add (length - i) to the total answer inside the loop, and apply % 10003 at each step to prevent overflow

name: N/3 Repeat Number - Boyer-Moore Algo
oneLiner: Use the Extended Boyer-Moore Voting Algorithm by maintaining two candidates and their counts; increment counts for matches, assign new candidates when counts are zero, and decrement both counts when a third distinct element is encountered. Always verify the final surviving candidates with a second pass to ensure their true frequency is strictly > N/3

name: Fractional Knapsack
oneLiner: Sort items descending by their value-to-weight ratio ((double) value / weight), greedily taking full items while capacity allows. If an item exceeds the remaining capacity, add the exact fractional value needed ((double) remaining_capacity * value / weight) to perfectly fill the bag, break the loop, and handle final floating-point precision if returning an integer

name: Unique Paths in a Grid
oneLiner: For all approaches, the core transition is summing the paths from the right and down cells, making sure to return 0 immediately if you hit a boundary or an obstacle. In Memoization (Top-Down), you simply cache these recursive results in a 2D array initialized to -1 to prevent redundant calculations. In Tabulation (Bottom-Up), after returning 0 if the start or end points are obstacles, set your destination dp[n-1][m-1] = 1 and loop backward through the grid, setting obstacle cells to 0 and all others to the sum of their adjacent right and bottom cells

name: Unique Binary Search Trees II
oneLiner: To find the total unique BSTs for i nodes, pick one node as the root. The remaining i-1 nodes must be split between the left and right subtrees. If j nodes go left, i-1-j nodes must go right. The total combinations for that specific root is the product of the subtrees: dp[j] * dp[i-1-j]. Sum this product for all possible root splits (j from 0 to i-1) to get dp[i]

name: Max Sum Without Adjacent Elements

name: N digit numbers

For your 1-digit base case, ensure you initialize dp[1][i] = 1 only up to Math.min(9, B) to prevent index out of bounds when the target sum is extremely small.

name: Count Subarrays- Unique

name: Fibonacci Number -DP
oneLiner: Execution: > 1. The Core Relation: State(N) = State(N-1) + State(N-2). 2. Top-Down (Memoization): Init array with -1. Base cases if (N <= 1) return N. Check cache if (dp[N] != -1). Recurse and save. Time: O(N), Space: O(N). 3. Bottom-Up (Tabulation): Base cases dp[0]=0, dp[1]=1. for(i=2 to N) dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2]. 4. Senior Optimization (Slider): We only ever need the last two states. Drop the array. Use a=0, b=1. Loop c = a + b, then shift a = b and b = c. Return c. Time: O(N), Space: O(1)

name: Stairs
oneLiner: Execution: > 1. The Core Relation: To reach step N, you either came from step N-1 or N-2. Therefore: ways(N) = (ways(N-1) + ways(N-2)) % 1000000007. 2. Top-Down (Memoization): Init array with -1. Base cases: if (N == 0 || N == 1) return 1;. Check cache: if (dp[N] != -1) return dp[N];. Recurse, modulo, and save. (Time: O(N), Space: O(N)). 3. Bottom-Up (Tabulation): Base cases dp[0]=1, dp[1]=1. Loop i = 2 to N: dp[i] = (dp[i-1] + dp[i-2]) % 1000000007. 4. Senior Optimization (Slider): We only need the last two steps. Use a=1, b=1. Loop from 2 to N: c = (a + b) % 1000000007, then shift a = b and b = c. Return c. (Time: O(N), Space: O(1))

name: Minimum Number of Squares
oneLiner: Execution: > 1. The Core Relation: State(N) = min( State(N - op*op) ) + 1 for all perfect squares op*op <= N.2. Top-Down (Memoization): Base case if (N == 0) return 0. Init ov_min = MAX_VALUE. Loop while (N - i*i >= 0). CRITICAL TRAP: Do not forget i++ inside the loop, or it will trigger an infinite loop and TLE!3. Bottom-Up (Tabulation): >    * Init dp array of size N + 1. Base cases: dp[0] = 0, dp[1] = 1.Outer loop: for (int i = 2; i <= n; i++) to build each target number.Inner loop: while (i - op*op >= 0).Calculate ans = dp[i - op*op] + 1. Update ov_min = Math.min(ov_min, ans).Increment op++.Save dp[i] = ov_min.Complexity: Time is O(N \sqrt{N}) because the inner loop only runs sqrt{i} times. Space is O(N)
